See “Non-GAAP Financial Measures” for a reconciliation of selected U.S. GAAP-based measures to adjusted measures for the three months ended August 31, 2025, May 31, 2025 and August 31, 2024.
U.S. GAAP Operating Expenses
Operating expenses decreased by $3.2 million sequentially, or 3.6%, in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, compared to the first quarter of fiscal 2026 primarily due to a decrease of $3.9 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $1.8 million in sales incentive plan costs, a decrease of $1.3 million in marketing expense and a decrease of $0.9 million in amortization costs, partially offset by an increase of $1.5 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs, an increase of $1.4 million in variable incentive plan costs, a decrease in benefit of $0.9 million relating to costs recovered under a transition service agreement related to the Company’s sale of Cylance in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 and an increase of $0.7 million in consulting costs.
Operating expenses decreased by $1.3 million year-over-year, or 1.5%, in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2025 primarily due to an increase in benefits of $3.8 million from SIF claims filed, a decrease of $1.6 million in infrastructure costs and a decrease of $1.5 million in amortization costs, partially offset by an increase of $2.5 million in restructuring costs, an increase of $1.0 million in salaries and benefits expense, an increase of $0.8 million in stock compensation expense and an increase of $0.6 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs.
Adjusted Operating Expenses
Adjusted operating expenses decreased by $3.6 million sequentially, or 4.6%, to $74.8 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to $78.4 million in the first quarter of fiscal 2026. The decrease was primarily due to a decrease of $3.9 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $1.8 million in sales incentive plan costs and a decrease of $1.3 million in marketing expense, partially offset by an increase of $1.5 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs, an increase of $1.4 million in variable incentive plan costs, a decrease in benefit of $0.9 million relating to costs recovered under a transition service agreement related to the Company’s sale of Cylance in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 and an increase of $0.7 million in consulting costs.
Adjusted operating expenses decreased by $3.7 million year-over-year, or 4.7%, to $74.8 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, compared to $78.5 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to an increase in benefits of $3.8 million from SIF claims filed, a decrease of $1.6 million in infrastructure costs and a decrease of $0.9 million in
48

amortization costs, partially offset by an increase of $1.0 million in salaries and benefits expense and an increase of $0.6 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs.
Adjusted Corporate operating costs is defined as the portion of the Company’s total adjusted operating costs that is not attributable to any of the three operating segments.
The Company previously stated that it expected adjusted Corporate operating costs excluding amortization to be approximately $10 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. Adjusted Corporate operating costs excluding amortization were $9.9 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026.
The Company continues to expect adjusted Corporate operating costs excluding amortization to be approximately $10 million in each of the third quarter and fourth quarter of fiscal 2026.
Research and Development Expenses
Research and development expenses consist primarily of salaries and benefits costs for technical personnel, new product development costs, travel expenses, office and building costs, infrastructure costs and other employee costs.
Research and development expenses decreased by $1.5 million, or 5.5%, in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2025 primarily due to an increase in benefits of $3.8 million from SIF claims filed, partially offset by an increase of $1.1 million in salaries and benefits expense and an increase of $0.3 million in variable incentive plan costs.
Adjusted research and development expenses decreased by $1.7 million, or 6.6%, to $24.2 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to $25.9 million the second quarter of fiscal 2025, primarily due to the same reasons described above on a U.S. GAAP basis.
Sales and Marketing Expenses
Sales and marketing expenses consist primarily of marketing, advertising and promotion, salaries and benefits, information technology costs and travel expenses.
Sales and marketing expenses increased by $3.1 million, or 14.6%, in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2025, primarily due to an increase of $1.5 million in salaries and benefits expense, an increase of $0.7 million in stock compensation expense and an increase of $0.2 million in marketing expense.
Adjusted sales and marketing expenses increased by $2.5 million, or 12.1%, to $23.1 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to $20.6 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The increase was primarily due to an increase of $1.5 million in salaries and benefits expense and an increase of $0.2 million in marketing expense.
General and Administrative Expenses
General and administrative expenses consist primarily of salaries and benefits, external advisory fees, information technology costs, office and related staffing infrastructure costs.
General and administrative expenses decreased by $1.3 million, or 4.0%, in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to a decrease of $1.6 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $1.5 million in infrastructure costs and a decrease of $1.4 million in facilities cost, partially offset by an increase of $2.5 million in restructuring costs and an increase of $0.6 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs.
Adjusted general and administrative expenses decreased by $3.6 million, or 12.4%, to $25.5 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to $29.1 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to a decrease of $1.6 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $1.5 million in infrastructure costs and a decrease of $1.4 million in facilities cost, partially offset by an increase of $0.6 million in the Company’s deferred share unit costs.
49

Amortization Expense
The table below presents a comparison of amortization expense relating to property, plant and equipment and intangible assets recorded as amortization or cost of sales for the quarter ended August 31, 2025 compared to the quarter ended August 31, 2024. Intangible assets are comprised of patents, licenses and acquired technology.

Amortization included in Operating Expense
The decrease in amortization expense included in operating expense of $1.5 million was primarily due to the lower cost base of assets.
Adjusted amortization expense decreased by $0.9 million to $2.0 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to $2.9 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025 primarily due to the lower cost base of assets.
Amortization included in Cost of Sales
Amortization expense relating to certain intangible assets employed in the Company’s licensing operations was $1.5 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 and was consistent with the second quarter of fiscal 2025.
Investment Income, Net
Investment income, net, which includes the interest expense from the Notes (as defined below), was $1.9 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 and decreased by $0.8 million from investment income, net of $2.7 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to interest income on a delayed tax refund in the second quarter of fiscal 2025 which did not recur, partially offset by a higher return on cash and investments due to the higher cash and investment balance.
Income Taxes
For the second quarter of fiscal 2026, the Company’s net effective income tax expense rate was approximately 1% (second quarter of fiscal 2025 - net effective income tax expense rate of approximately 8%). The Company’s net effective income tax rate reflects the change in unrecognized income tax benefits, if any, and the fact that the Company has a significant valuation allowance against its deferred tax assets; in particular, any change in loss carry forwards or research and development credits, amongst other items, was offset by a corresponding adjustment of the valuation allowance. The Company’s net effective income tax rate also reflects the geographic mix of earnings in jurisdictions with different income tax rates.
Net Income (Loss)
The Company’s net income for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 was $13.3 million, or $0.02 basic and diluted earnings per share on a U.S. GAAP basis (second quarter of fiscal 2025 - net loss $19.7 million, or $0.03 basic and diluted loss per share). The period over period change of $33.0 million was primarily due to a loss from discontinued operations in the second quarter of fiscal 2025 which did not recur, an increase in gross margin percentage, as described above in “Consolidated Gross Margin Percentage”, an increase in revenue, as described above in “Revenue by Segment” and a decrease in operating expenses, as described above in “Operating Expenses”.
Adjusted net income was $24.2 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026, or $0.04 adjusted basic earnings per share (second quarter of fiscal 2025 - adjusted net loss of $2.6 million, or 0.00 adjusted basic loss per share). The increase in adjusted net income of $26.8 million was primarily due to the same reasons described above on a U.S. GAAP basis.
50

The Company previously stated that it expected adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $8 to $14 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. Adjusted EBITDA was $25.9 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 due to QNX and Secure Communications exceeding the top end of their expected adjusted EBITDA ranges as discussed above in “Adjusted Gross Margin and Adjusted EBITDA by Segment”.
The Company previously stated that it expected non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of breakeven to $0.01 in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. Non-GAAP EPS was $0.04 in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 primarily due to QNX and Secure Communications exceeding the top end of their expected adjusted EBITDA ranges as discussed above in “Adjusted Gross Margin and Adjusted EBITDA by Segment”.
The Company previously stated that it expected operating cash usage to be in the range of $5 million to $15 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026. Operating cash flow was $3.4 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 due to better than expected conversion of profit into cash.
The Company expects adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $20 million to $28 million in the third quarter of fiscal 2026. The Company previously stated that it expected adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $72 million to $87 million in fiscal 2026 as a whole. The Company now expects adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $82 million to $101 million in fiscal 2026 as a whole due to stronger than expected performance in the first half of fiscal 2026, as described above in “Adjusted Gross Margin and Adjusted EBITDA by Segment”.
The Company expects non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of $0.02 per share to $0.04 per share in the third quarter of fiscal 2026. The Company previously stated that it expected non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of $0.08 per share to $0.10 per share in fiscal 2026 as a whole. The Company now expects non-GAAP EPS to be in the range of $0.11 per share to $0.15 per share in fiscal 2026 as a whole due to stronger than expected performance in the first half of fiscal 2026.
The Company expects operating cash flow to be in the range of $10 million to $20 million in the third quarter of fiscal 2026. The Company previously stated that it expected operating cash flow to be approximately $35 million for fiscal 2026 as a whole. The Company now expects operating cash flow to be in the range of $35 million to $40 million for fiscal 2026 as a whole due to stronger than expected operating cash flow in the first half of fiscal 2026.
The Company does not provide a reconciliation of expected adjusted EBITDA and expected non-GAAP basic EPS for the third quarter of fiscal year 2026, and for fiscal 2026 as a whole, to the most directly comparable expected GAAP measures because it is unable to predict with reasonable certainty, among other things, restructuring charges and impairment charges and, accordingly, a reconciliation is not available without unreasonable effort. These items are uncertain, depend on various factors, and could have a material impact on GAAP reported results for the guidance period.
The weighted average number of shares outstanding was 592.9 million common shares for basic earnings per share and 597.4 million common shares for diluted earnings per share for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 (second quarter of fiscal 2025 - 590.5 million common shares for basic and 591.6 million common shares for diluted loss per share). The weighted average number of shares outstanding includes the cumulative effects of share repurchases pursuant to the NCIB (as defined below) share buyback program during the quarter ended August 31, 2025.
51

See Note 8 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for the Company’s calculation of the diluted weighted average number of shares outstanding.
Revenue
Revenue by Segment
Comparative breakdowns of revenue by segment are set forth below.
 For the Six Months Ended
(in millions)

Revenue by Segment
QNX$120.6 $107.9 $12.7 
Secure Communications119.4 130.7 (11.3)

$251.3 $249.6 $1.7

52

QNX
The increase in QNX revenue of $12.7 million was primarily due to an increase of $6.0 million in development seat revenue, an increase of $5.4 million in royalty revenue and an increase of $3.8 million in BlackBerry Radar revenue, partially offset by a decrease of $2.4 million in professional services revenue.
Secure Communications
The decrease in Secure Communications revenue of $11.3 million primarily due to a decrease of $8.5 million in Secusmart product revenue and a decrease of $3.7 million in BlackBerry UEM license revenue, partially offset by an increase of $1.3 million in professional services revenue.
Licensing
The increase in Licensing revenue of $0.3 million was primarily due to an increase in revenue from the Company’s intellectual property licensing arrangements.
U.S. GAAP Revenue by Geography
Comparative breakdowns of the geographic regions on a U.S. GAAP basis are set forth in the following table:
 For the Six Months Ended
(in millions)
 August 31, 2025August 31, 2024Change
Revenue by Geography
North America$117.1 $114.4 $2.7 
Europe, Middle East and Africa82.4 87.3 (4.9)
Other regions51.8 47.9 3.9 
$251.3 $249.6 $1.7 
% Revenue by Geography
North America46.6 %45.8 %
Europe, Middle East and Africa32.8 %35.0 %
Other regions20.6 %19.2 %
100.0 %100.0 %
North America Revenue
The increase in North America revenue of $2.7 million was primarily due to an increase of $5.6 million in BlackBerry QNX development seat revenue and an increase of $3.7 million in BlackBerry Radar revenue, partially offset by a decrease of $4.7 million in BlackBerry QNX royalty revenue and a decrease of $2.6 million in BlackBerry UEM revenue.
Europe, Middle East and Africa Revenue
The decrease in Europe, Middle East and Africa revenue of $4.9 million was primarily due to a decrease of $8.6 million relating to product revenue in Secusmart, partially offset by an increase of $2.3 million BlackBerry QNX development seat revenue and an increase of $2.2 million in BlackBerry QNX royalty revenue.
Other Regions Revenue
The increase in other regions of $3.9 million was primarily due to an increase of $7.8 million relating to BlackBerry QNX royalty revenue, partially offset by a decrease of $1.9 million in BlackBerry QNX development seat revenue, a decrease of $0.7 million in professional services revenue and a decrease of $0.6 million in BlackBerry UEM revenue.
53

Consolidated Gross Margin
Consolidated gross margin increased by $8.3 million to approximately $186.9 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 (first six months of fiscal 2025 - $178.6 million). The increase was primarily due to an increase in revenue from QNX due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment”, partially offset by a decrease in revenue from BlackBerry UEM revenue, as the cost of sales for most software and services products does not significantly fluctuate based on business volume.
Consolidated Gross Margin Percentage
Consolidated gross margin percentage increased by 2.8%, to approximately 74.4% of consolidated revenue in the first six months of fiscal 2026 (first six months of fiscal 2025 - 71.6%). The increase was primarily due to a change in mix, specifically a higher relative gross margin contribution from Secusmart and QNX.
Adjusted Gross Margin and adjusted EBITDA by Segment
See “Business Overview” and “Second Quarter Fiscal 2026 Summary Results of Operations” for information about the Company’s operating segments and the basis of operating segment results.
For the Six Months Ended
 (in millions)

August 31,ChangeAugust 31,ChangeAugust 31,ChangeAugust 31,Change

QNX
The increase in QNX adjusted gross margin of $9.6 million was primarily due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment”, partially offset by an increase in cost of sales related to Radar hardware devices.
The decrease in QNX adjusted gross margin percentage of 1% was primarily due to the same reasons discussed above.
The increase in QNX adjusted EBITDA of $11.2 million was primarily due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment” and a benefit from SIF claims, partially offset by an increase in costs of sales related to Radar hardware devices and an increase in salaries and benefits expense. The Company does not expect to receive additional benefits from SIF claims in the second half of fiscal 2026.
Secure Communications
The decrease of Secure Communications adjusted gross margin of $1.7 million was primarily due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment”.
The increase in Secure Communications adjusted gross margin percentage of 5% was primarily due to a change in mix, specifically an increased gross margin contribution from Secusmart software licenses.
The increase of Secure Communications adjusted EBITDA of $2.1 million was primarily due to a decrease in salaries and benefits expense, partially offset by a decrease in revenue due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment”.
54

Licensing
The increase in Licensing adjusted gross margin of $0.2 million was primarily due to the reasons discussed above in “Revenue by Segment”.
Licensing adjusted gross margin percentage of 73% in the first six months of fiscal 2026 was consistent with the first six months of fiscal 2025.
The increase in Licensing adjusted EBITDA of $0.7 million was primarily due to the same reason discussed above, partially offset by a decrease in legal expense.
Operating Expenses
The table below presents a comparison of research and development, selling, marketing and administration, and amortization expense for the six months ended August 31, 2025, compared to the six months ended August 31, 2024.

See “Non-GAAP Financial Measures” for a reconciliation of selected U.S. GAAP-based measures to adjusted measures for the six months ended August 31, 2025 and August 31, 2024.
U.S. GAAP Operating Expenses
Operating expenses decreased by $15.9 million, or 8.4%, in the first six months of fiscal 2026, compared to the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to an increase in benefits of $8.3 million from SIF claims filed, a decrease of $3.5 million in impairment of long-lived asset impairment, a decrease of $2.2 million in amortization expense, a decrease of $2.2 million in infrastructure costs and a decrease of $1.9 million in restructuring cost, partially offset by an increase of $0.9 million in sales incentive plan cost and an increase of $0.8 million in stock compensation expense.
55

Adjusted Operating Expenses
Adjusted operating expenses decreased by $10.1 million, or 6.2%, to $153.2 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026, compared to $163.3 million the first six months of 2025. The decrease was primarily due to an increase in benefits of $8.3 million from SIF claims filed and a decrease of $2.2 million in infrastructure costs, partially offset by an increase of $0.9 million in sales incentive plan cost.
Research and Development Expenses
Research and development expenses consist primarily of salaries and benefits for technical personnel, new product development costs, travel, office and building costs, infrastructure costs and other employee costs.
Research and development expenses decreased by $7.1 million, or 12.3%, in the first six months of fiscal 2026, compared to the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to an increase in benefits of $8.3 million from SIF claims filed, partially offset by an increase of $0.4 million in salaries and benefits expense.
Adjusted research and development expenses decreased by $6.8 million, or 12.4%, to $47.9 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026, compared to $54.7 million in the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to the same reasons described above on a U.S. GAAP basis.
Sales and Marketing Expenses
Sales and marketing expenses consist primarily of marketing, advertising and promotion, salaries and benefits, information technology costs and travel expenses.
Sales and marketing expenses increased by $8.0 million, or 17.7%, in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to the first six months of fiscal 2025. The increase was primarily due to an increase of $4.0 million in salaries and benefits cost, an increase of $1.2 million in stock compensation expense, an increase of $1.0 million in sales incentive plan cost, an increase of $0.6 million in variable incentive plan cost and an increase of $0.6 million in marketing cost.
Adjusted sales and marketing expenses increased by $6.8 million, or 15.6%, to $50 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to $43.6 million in the first six months of fiscal 2025. The increase was primarily due to an increase of $4.0 million in salaries and benefits expense, an increase of $1.0 million in sales incentive plan cost, an increase of $0.6 million in variable incentive plan cost and an increase of $0.6 million in marketing cost.
General and Administrative Expenses
General and administration expenses consist primarily of salaries and benefits, external advisory fees, information technology costs, office and related staffing infrastructure costs.
General and administrative expenses decreased by $11.1 million, or 15.2%, in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to a decrease of $3.3 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $2.6 million in facilities cost, a decrease of $2.2 million in infrastructure costs, a decrease of $1.9 million in restructuring cost and a benefit of $1.1 million relating to costs recovered under a transition service agreement related to the Company’s sale of Cylance in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025.
Adjusted general and administrative expenses decreased by $8.6 million, or 14.5%, to $50.6 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to $59.2 million in the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to a decrease of $3.3 million in salaries and benefits expense, a decrease of $2.6 million in facilities cost, a decrease of $2.2 million in infrastructure costs and a benefit of $1.1 million relating to costs recovered under a transition service agreement related to the Company’s sale of Cylance in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025.
Amortization Expense
The table below presents a comparison of amortization expense relating to property, plant and equipment and intangible assets recorded as amortization or cost of sales for the six months ended August 31, 2025 compared to the six months ended August 31, 2024. Intangible assets are comprised of patents, licenses and acquired technology.
56

(in millions)

Amortization included in Operating Expense
The decrease in amortization expense included in operating expense of $2.2 million was primarily due to the lower cost base of assets.
Adjusted amortization expense decreased by $1.5 million to $4.3 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to $5.8 million in the first six months of fiscal 2025 due to the same reasons described above.
Amortization included in Cost of Sales
Amortization expense relating to certain property, plant and equipment and certain intangible assets employed in the Company’s service operations decreased by $0.1 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 compared to the first six months of fiscal 2025 due to a lower cost base of assets.
Investment Income, Net
Investment income, net, which includes the interest expense from the Notes, was $4.8 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 and decreased by $1.9 million from $6.7 million in the first six months of fiscal 2025. The decrease was primarily due to unrealized gains recognized from observable price changes on non-marketable equity investments without readily determinable fair value in the first six months of fiscal 2025 which did not recur, interest income on a delayed tax refund in the first six months of fiscal 2025 which did not recur, partially offset by a higher return on cash and investments due to the higher cash and investment balance.
Income Taxes
For the first six months of fiscal 2026, the Company’s net effective income tax expense rate was approximately 17% (first six months of fiscal 2025 - net effective income tax expense rate of approximately 17%). The Company’s net effective income tax rate reflects the change in unrecognized income tax benefits, if any, and the fact that the Company has a significant valuation allowance against its deferred tax assets, and in particular, the change in loss carry forwards, research and development credits, amongst other items, was offset by a corresponding adjustment of the valuation allowance. The Company’s net effective income tax rate also reflects the geographic mix of earnings in jurisdictions with different income tax rates.
Net Income (loss)
The Company’s net income for the first six months of fiscal 2026 was $15.2 million, or $0.03 basic and diluted earnings per share on a U.S. GAAP basis (first six months of fiscal 2025 - net loss $61.1 million, or $0.10 basic and diluted loss per share). The increase in net income of $76.3 million was primarily due to a loss from discontinued operations in the first six months of fiscal 2025 which did not recur, a decrease in operating expenses, as described above in “Operating Expenses”, an increase in gross margin percentage, as described above in “Consolidated Gross Margin Percentage” and an increase in revenue as described above in “Revenue by Segment”.
Adjusted net income was $36.5 million in the first six months of fiscal 2026 or $0.06 adjusted basic earnings per share (first six months of fiscal 2025 - adjusted net loss of $16.9 million, or $0.03 adjusted basic loss per share). The increase in adjusted net income of $53.4 million was primarily due to the same reasons described above on a U.S. GAAP basis.
57

The weighted average number of shares outstanding was 595 million for basic earnings per share and 599 million for basis diluted earnings per share for the first six months of August 31, 2025. The weighted average number of shares outstanding was 590 million for basic and diluted loss per share for the first six months of August 31, 2024.
Common Shares Outstanding
On September 22, 2025, there were 590 million voting common shares, 17.6 million restricted share units and 1.5 million deferred share units outstanding. In addition, 51.5 million common shares are issuable upon conversion in full of the Notes as described in Note 6 to the Consolidated Financial Statements.
The Company has not paid any cash dividends during the last three fiscal years. 
Financial Condition
Liquidity and Capital Resources
Cash, cash equivalents, and investments decreased by $46.8 million to $363.5 million as at August 31, 2025 from $410.3 million as at February 28, 2025, primarily due to changes in working capital.
A comparative summary of cash, cash equivalents, and investments is set out below:
As at
(in millions)

Cash and cash equivalents$276.4 $266.7 $9.7 
Restricted cash and cash equivalents14.2 13.6 0.6

Long-term investments (1)
58.8 58.9 (0.1)
Cash, cash equivalents, and investments$363.5 $410.3 $(46.8)
______________________________
(1)Includes equity investments in private companies, including common shares of Arctic Wolf that were received as partial consideration for the sale of its Cylance endpoint security assets and liabilities to Arctic Wolf. Equity investments in private companies are considered illiquid securities without a public market and, as such, they cannot be readily sold or exchanged for cash.
The table below summarizes the current assets, current liabilities, and working capital of the Company:
As at
(in millions)
 August 31, 2025February 28, 2025Change
Current assets$507.2 $591.5 $(84.3)
Current liabilities230.2 344.3 (114.1)
Working capital$277.0 $247.2 $29.8 
Current Assets
The decrease in current assets of $84.3 million at the end of the second quarter of fiscal 2026 from the end of the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 was primarily due to a decrease of $57.0 million in short term investments, a decrease of $48.7 million in accounts receivable, net of allowance, partially offset by an increase of $10.4 million in other current assets, an increase of $9.7 million in cash and cash equivalents, an increase of $1.0 million in income taxes receivable and an increase of $0.3 million in other receivables.
At August 31, 2025, accounts receivable, net of allowance was $125 million, a decrease of $48.7 million from February 28, 2025. The decrease was primarily due to the collection of a significant receivable from the Government of Malaysia and a decrease in days sales outstanding to 90 days at the end of the second quarter of fiscal 2026 from 102 days at the end of the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, partially offset by a reclassification of a licensing receivable from long-term receivable to accounts receivable.
At August 31, 2025, other current assets were $40.4 million, an increase of $10.4 million from February 28, 2025. The increase was primarily due to an increase of $5.8 million in prepaid professional services and an increase of $2.0 million in prepaid software.
58

At August 31, 2025, income taxes receivable were $2.6 million, an increase of $1 million from February 28, 2025. The increase was primarily due to tax installments and prepayments required in certain taxable jurisdictions.
At August 31, 2025, other receivables were $48.7 million, an increase of $0.3 million from February 28, 2025. The increase was primarily due to an increase of $1.5 million in SIF claims, partially offset by a decrease of $0.5 million in sublease receivable.
Current Liabilities
The decrease in current liabilities of $114.1 million at the end of the second quarter of 2026 from the end of the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 was primarily due to a decrease of $42.3 million in accrued liabilities, a decrease of $33 million in deferred revenue, current, a decrease of $27.1 million in accounts payable and a decrease of $11.7 million in income taxes payable.
Accrued liabilities were $83.9 million at the end of the second quarter of 2026, reflecting a decrease of $42.3 million compared to February 28, 2025, which was primarily due to a decrease of $14.6 million in variable incentive plan accrual, a decrease of $4.8 million in restructuring cost accrual, a decrease of $4.2 million in litigation accruals, a decrease of $3.8 million in operating lease liability, current, a decrease of $2.0 million in deferred share unit liability, a decrease of $1.7 million in professional services accrual, a decrease of $1.6 million related to a lease termination fee accrual, a decrease of $1.4 million in inventory accrual and a decrease of $1.0 million in HST accruals related to the Company’s sale of Cylance in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025.
Deferred revenue, current was $128.5 million, which reflects a decrease of $33.0 million compared to February 28, 2025 that was attributable to a decrease of $28.5 million in deferred revenue, current related to BlackBerry UEM, a decrease of $4.0 million in deferred revenue, current related to QNX and a decrease of $2.8 million in deferred revenue, current related to BlackBerry AtHoc.
Accounts payable were $4.0 million, reflecting a decrease of $27.1 million from February 28, 2025, which was primarily due to the timing of payments.
Income taxes payable were $13.8 million, reflecting a decrease of $11.7 million from February 28, 2025, which was primarily due to changes in the quarterly tax provision and tax payments.
Cash flows for the six months ended August 31, 2025 compared to the six months ended August 31, 2024 were as follows:
For the Six Months Ended
(in millions)
 August 31, 2025August 31, 2024Change
Net cash flows provided by (used in):
Operating activities$(14.1)$(31.1)$17.0 
Investing activities52.8 16.6 36.2 
Financing activities(28.8)1.5 (30.3)
Effect of foreign exchange loss on cash and cash equivalents0.4 0.2 0.2 
Net increase (decrease) in cash and cash equivalents$10.3 $(12.8)$23.1 
Operating Activities
The decrease in net cash flows used in operating activities of $17 million primarily reflects the changes in working capital.
Investing Activities
During the six months ended August 31, 2025, cash flows provided by investing activities were $52.8 million and included cash provided by transactions involving the acquisition of short-term and long-term investments, net of the proceeds on sale or maturity in the amount of $57.1 million, offset by cash used in the acquisition of intangible assets of $2.6 million and the acquisition of property, plant and equipment of $1.7 million. For the same period in the prior fiscal year, cash flows provided by investing activities were $16.6 million and included cash used in transactions involving the acquisitions of short-term and long-term investments, net of the proceeds on sale or maturity in the amount of $22.2 million, offset by cash used in the acquisition of intangible assets of $3.8 million, the acquisition of property, plant and equipment $1.9 million, and the proceeds on sale of property, plant and equipment of $0.1 million.
59

Financing Activities
The increase in cash flows used in financing activities was $30.3 million for the first six months of fiscal 2026 primarily due to a repurchase of common shares of $30.0 million pursuant to the NCIB share buyback program and a decrease of $0.3 million in common shares issued upon the exercise of stock options and under the employee share purchase plan.
On May 8, 2025, the Company announced that it received acceptance from the Toronto Stock Exchange with respect to a normal course issuer bid share buyback program to purchase for cancellation up to 27,855,153 common shares of the Company, or approximately 4.7% of the outstanding public float at May 5, 2025. The NCIB share buyback program commenced on May 12, 2025, and will terminate on the earliest of May 11, 2026, such date as the Company may determine, or the date on which the maximum number of common shares that may be purchased under the NCIB share buyback program has been reached. The Company is not obligated to repurchase any common shares under the NCIB share buyback program. During the six months ended August 31, 2025, the Company repurchased 7.6 million common shares at a cost of $30.0 million.
Debt Financing and Other Funding Sources
See Note 6 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for a description of the Company’s $200 million aggregate principal amount of 3.00% senior convertible unsecured notes issued in January 2024 (the “Notes”).
The Company has $14.1 million in collateralized outstanding letters of credit in support of certain leasing arrangements entered into in the ordinary course of business. See Note 3 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for further information concerning the Company’s restricted cash.
Cash, cash equivalents, and investments were $363.5 million as at August 31, 2025. The Company’s management remains focused on maintaining appropriate cash balances, efficiently managing working capital balances and managing the liquidity needs of the business. Based on its current financial projections, the Company believes its financial resources, together with expected future operating cash generating and operating expense reduction activities, should be sufficient to meet funding requirements for current financial commitments and future operating expenditures not yet committed, and should provide the necessary financial capacity for the foreseeable future.
Contractual and Other Obligations
The following table sets out aggregate information about the Company’s contractual and other obligations and the periods in which payments are due as at August 31, 2025:
 (in millions)

(>12 months)

Total contractual and other obligations as at August 31, 2025 increased by $0.5 million as compared to the February 28, 2025 balance of $312.8 million, which was attributable to an increase in purchase obligations and commitments, partially offset by a decrease in operating lease obligations.
The Company does not have any material off-balance sheet arrangements.
Accounting Policies and Critical Accounting Estimates
There have been no changes to the Company’s accounting policies or critical accounting estimates from those described under “Accounting Policies and Critical Accounting Estimates” in the Annual MD&A.
ITEM 3. QUANTITATIVE AND QUALITATIVE DISCLOSURES ABOUT MARKET RISK
The Company is engaged in operating and financing activities that generate risk in three primary areas:
Foreign Exchange
The Company is exposed to foreign exchange risk as a result of transactions in currencies other than its functional currency, the U.S. dollar. The majority of the Company’s revenue in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 was transacted in U.S. dollars. Portions of the revenue were denominated in Canadian dollars, euros and British pounds. Expenses, consisting mainly of salaries and certain other operating costs, were incurred primarily in Canadian dollars, but were also incurred in U.S. dollars, euros and British pounds. At August 31, 2025, approximately 10% of cash and cash equivalents, 25% of accounts receivables and 71% of accounts payable were denominated in foreign currencies (February 28, 2025 – 19.0%, 29% and 71%, respectively). These
60

foreign currencies primarily include the Canadian dollar, euro and British pound. As part of its risk management strategy, the Company maintains net monetary asset and/or liability balances in foreign currencies and engages in foreign currency hedging activities using derivative financial instruments, including currency forward contracts and currency options. The Company does not use derivative instruments for speculative purposes. If overall foreign currency exchange rates to the U.S. dollar uniformly weakened or strengthened by 10% related to the Company’s net monetary asset or liability balances in foreign currencies at August 31, 2025 (after hedging activities), the impact to the Company would be immaterial.
The Company regularly reviews its currency forward and option positions, both on a stand-alone basis and in conjunction with its underlying foreign currency exposures. Given the effective horizons of the Company’s risk management activities and the anticipatory nature of the exposures, there can be no assurance these positions will offset more than a portion of the financial impact resulting from movements in currency exchange rates. Further, the recognition of the gains and losses related to these instruments may not coincide with the timing of gains and losses related to the underlying economic exposures and, therefore, may adversely affect the Company’s financial condition and operating results.
Interest Rate
Cash and cash equivalents and investments are invested in certain instruments with fixed interest rates of varying maturities. Consequently, the Company is exposed to interest rate risk as a result of holding investments of varying maturities and the significant financing components within certain revenue contracts with customers. The fair value of investments, as well as the investment income derived from the investment portfolio, will fluctuate with changes in prevailing interest rates. The Company also has significant financing components within certain revenue contracts with customers and is exposed to interest rate risk as a result of discounting the future payments from customers with a fixed interest rate. The Company also has outstanding Notes with a fixed interest rate, as described in Note 6 to the Consolidated Financial Statements. The Company is exposed to interest rate risk as a result of the Notes. The Company does not currently utilize interest rate derivative instruments.
Credit and Customer Concentration
The Company, in the normal course of business, monitors the financial condition of its customers and reviews the credit history of each new customer. The Company establishes an allowance for credit losses (“ACL”) that corresponds to the specific credit risk of its customers, historical trends and economic circumstances. The ACL as at August 31, 2025 was $5.4 million (February 28, 2025 - $6.6 million). There were two customers that comprised more than 10% of accounts receivable as at August 31, 2025 (February 28, 2025 - two customers that comprised more than 10%). During the second quarter of fiscal 2026, the percentage of the Company’s receivable balance that was past due decreased by 14.7% compared to the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025. Although the Company actively monitors and attempts to collect on its receivables as they become due, the risk of further delays or challenges in obtaining timely payments of receivables from resellers and other distribution partners exists. The occurrence of such delays or challenges in obtaining timely payments could negatively impact the Company’s liquidity and financial condition. There was no customer that comprised more than 10% of the Company’s revenue and one customer that comprised 11% of the Company’s revenue in the three and six months ended August 31, 2025, respectively (three and six months ended August 31, 2024 - one customer that comprised 13% of the Company’s revenue and one customer that comprised 14% of the Company’s revenue, respectively).
Market values are determined for each individual security in the investment portfolio. The Company assesses declines in the value of individual investments for impairment to determine whether the decline is other-than-temporary. The Company makes this assessment by considering available evidence including changes in general market conditions, specific industry and individual company data, the length of time and the extent to which the fair value has been less than cost, the financial condition, the near-term prospects of the individual investment and the Company’s ability and intent to hold the debt securities to maturity.
ITEM 4. CONTROLS AND PROCEDURES
As of August 31, 2025, the Company carried out an evaluation, under the supervision and with the participation of the Company’s management, including the Company’s Chief Executive Officer and its Chief Financial Officer, of the effectiveness of the design and operation of the Company’s disclosure controls and procedures, as defined in Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) under the Exchange Act. Based on that evaluation, the Chief Executive Officer and the Chief Financial Officer have concluded that, as of such date, the Company’s disclosure controls and procedures were effective to give reasonable assurance that the information required to be disclosed by the Company in reports that it files or submits under the Exchange Act is (i) recorded, processed, summarized and reported, within the time periods specified in the SEC’s rules and forms, and (ii) accumulated and communicated to management, including its principal executive and principal financial officers, or persons performing similar functions, as appropriate to allow timely decisions regarding required disclosure.
61

Changes in Internal Control Over Financial Reporting
During the three months ended August 31, 2025, no changes were made to the Company’s internal control over financial reporting that have materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, the Company’s internal control over financial reporting.
PART II - OTHER INFORMATION
ITEM 1. LEGAL PROCEEDINGS
See Note 10 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for information regarding certain legal proceedings in which the Company is involved.
ITEM 2. UNREGISTERED SALES OF EQUITY SECURITIES AND USE OF PROCEEDS
Purchases of Equity Securities by the Issuer and Affiliated Purchasers
PeriodTotal Number of Shares PurchasedAverage Price Paid per ShareTotal Number of Shares Purchased as Part of Publicly Announced Plans or ProgramsMaximum Number of Shares That May Yet Be Purchased Under the Plans or Programs
June 1, 2025 - June 30, 20250N/AN/AN/A
July 1, 2025 - July 31, 20252,387,128$4.192,387,12822,898,066 
Aug 1, 2025 - Aug 31, 20252,637,798$3.772,637,79820,260,268 
On May 8, 2025, the Company announced the initiation of a normal course issuer bid (“NCIB”) share buyback program, which was approved by the Toronto Stock Exchange. The program allows for the repurchase of up to 27,855,153 of the Company’s common shares, representing approximately 4.7% of the outstanding public float as of the close of business on May 5, 2025.
The NCIB share buyback program commenced on May 12, 2025, and will terminate on the earliest of May 11, 2026, such date as the Company may determine, or the date on which the maximum number of common shares that may be purchased under the NCIB share buyback program has been reached.
Purchases under the NCIB share buyback program may be made through the Toronto Stock Exchange, other Canadian stock exchanges, the New York Stock Exchange, and/or alternative trading systems in Canada and the United States. Subject to regulatory approval, purchases may also be made by the Company by way of private agreements or share repurchase programs under issuer bid exemption orders issued by securities regulatory authorities.
Any common shares purchased through the NCIB share buyback program will be cancelled.
ITEM 5. OTHER INFORMATION
Rule 10b5-1 and Non-Rule 10b5-1 Trading Arrangements
During the three months ended August 31, 2025, none of the officers or directors of the Company adopted or terminated trading arrangements for the purchase or sale of the Company’s common shares.
